answer:
**Iqbal's use of *qaum* (قوم), commonly translated here as "nation," appears in the original Urdu texts of *Bang-e-Dara* (primarily in transliterations), rendered in English translations as "nation," "people," or contextually as a collective community bound by shared purpose, likened to a living body (*jism*) with individuals (*afraad*) as its limbs (*aaza*).[^1][^2] It carries connotations of organic unity, collective vitality, and destiny, but Iqbal evolves and critiques it across the collection, distinguishing territorial or Western-style nationalism from the universal Islamic *millat* (community/faith-nation). The term is not exhaustively defined in a single passage but traced through poetic exemplars, often urging awakening, action, and subordination to faith.**

### Early Patriotic Echoes (Part 1)
In Part 1, *qaum* emerges in a national-patriotic mode, as in the title of Chapter 26, "Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet" ("National Anthem for Indian Children"), celebrating India's historical and spiritual landscape as "my homeland" (*meri dharti*), blending local pride with Islamic references (e.g., Chishti, Nanak, Hijaz).[^3] This reflects Iqbal's early 1920s context amid Indian nationalism.

A foundational metaphor appears in Chapter 39, "Shair" ("The Poet"):  
> The Nation [*Qaum*] is a body, a living frame,  
> Its people are the limbs that bear its name.  
> Its hands and feet tread paths of craft and trade,  
> Its government, a graceful face displayed.  
> The Poet is the Nation’s seeing eye;  
> When one part aches, it cannot help but cry.[^4]

Here, *qaum* is an organic entity (*jism*), interdependent and sentient, with the poet as its empathetic "eye" (*deeda-e-beena-e-qaum*), sensing collective pain.[^5]

In Chapter 38, "Sayed Ki Loh-e-Turbat" ("The Epitaph of the Sayed"), a tombstone inscription warns: "Do not teach your own people [*qaum*] to cast the world aside" (*Tark-e duniya qaum ko apni na sikhlana*), prioritizing worldly engagement over ascetic withdrawal for communal strength.[^6]

### Islamic Universalism and Critique (Part 3)
By Part 3, *qaum* aligns more explicitly with the Muslim *millat*, critiquing narrow nationalism. Chapter 23, "Tarana-e-Milli" ("The Muslim Anthem"), evokes a borderless Muslim *qaum*: "From Chin to Arab lands, our story flows, / The whole wide world's the home a Muslim knows," emphasizing faith (*One God*) over geography.[^7]

Chapter 4, the expansive "Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai," repeatedly invokes *qaum* (e.g., "Waa'iz-e-qaum" as "preacher of the nation"): "A nation has a faith; if not, then what are you?" (*Qaum mazhab se hai, mazhab jo nahin, tum bhi nahin*), tying existence to religion.[^8] It laments the *qaum*'s decline: preachers lack "sharp and seasoned thought" (*pukhta-khayaali*), urging revival through prophetic unity.[^9]

The critique peaks in Chapter 25, "Wataniyat" ("Nationalism"), where *qaum*/*nation* is a modern "idol" (*but*) desecrating faith:  
> Of all these newer gods, the greatest is the Nation;  
> The robe it wears is Faith’s own final desecration.  
> ...  
> Your homeland is Islam, you serve the Prophet’s will.  
> ...  
> In the discourse of the state, the Nation is but land;  
> In the Prophet’s teaching, Homeland is by God’s command.[^10]

*Qalum* thus risks fragmenting Islamic brotherhood (*ukhuwwat*), sowing rivalry; true belonging is to the *millat-e-Islam* ("Nation of Islam"), fluid like ocean-fish, not land-bound.[^11]

### Across the Cycle (Parts 1–3)
No direct *qaum* usages appear in Part 2 searches, but the arc shifts from Indian *qaumi* patriotism (Part 1) to organic metaphor, then pan-Islamic *millat* (Part 3), rejecting secular nationalism as a Western import eroding unity. Iqbal's *qaum* demands action (*koshish*), faith-integration, and selfhood (*khudi*), warning against stasis or division.[^12] The corpus lacks a prosaic treatise-definition, focusing poetic evocation; fuller elaboration appears in later works like *Rumuz-e-Bekhudi* (not in this corpus).

[^1]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 39 "Shair," transliteration: "Qaum goya jism hai, afraad hain aaza-e-qaum," /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/39-shair/transliteration/#p-4d6d95.

[^2]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 39 "Shair," translation: "The Nation is a body," /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/39-shair/translation/#p-4562af.

[^3]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 26, translation, /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/.

[^4]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 39, translation, /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/39-shair/translation/#p-4562af.

[^5]: Ibid., transliteration #p-4d6d95.

[^6]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 38, transliteration snippet (para p-e91825), /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/38-sayed-ki-loh-e-turbat/transliteration/.

[^7]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 23 "Tarana-e-Milli," translation, /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/23-tarana-e-milli/translation/#p-8a4681.

[^8]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 4, translation #p-7a9795.

[^9]: Ibid., transliteration snippets (e.g., p-79c435), /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/transliteration/#p-79c435.

[^10]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 25 "Wataniyat," translation #p-79b1e9 and #p-a723b2.

[^11]: Ibid., #p-97937b.

[^12]: Cross-ref. Ch. 13 "Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam," on ancestral *qaum*, /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462.
